_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: As of June 2026, WTI crude is priced at approximately $79.2 per barrel, Brent crude at $81.62 per barrel, and US regular gasoline at $3.85 per gallon. Henry Hub natural gas is quoted at $2.83 per MMBtu. Indexed to 2019 levels (set at 100), natural gas has exhibited the highest volatility among these fuels, experiencing a pronounced spike in 2022 followed by a decline. In contrast, crude oil and gasoline prices have generally moved more in tandem, reflecting a different market dynamic and supply-demand sensitivity for natural gas.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which represents the average total cost to build and operate a power-generating asset per megawatt-hour (MWh) of electricity produced, shows solar PV at $44/MWh and onshore wind at $33/MWh as the cheapest new options globally. These costs now undercut those of new coal and gas plants, which stand at $110/MWh and $76/MWh respectively. Offshore wind sits at $75/MWh, slightly above gas but below coal. Solar PV's levelised cost has declined approximately 90% since 2010—from around $460/MWh to $44/MWh—reflecting technological advances and economies of scale, markedly improving its competitiveness.
notes.currency: An estimated 20% of global oil trade is now settled outside the US dollar, and this share is increasing, although no official statistical measure exists for this figure. Despite this gradual shift, the US dollar remains the dominant currency for oil trading globally. Key drivers of the move away from dollar settlement include Russia's increased trade with Asian buyers and transactions settled in yuan and rupees, reflecting evolving geopolitical and economic relationships.
notes.now: Current energy markets exhibit a complex interplay between fossil fuel prices, renewable electricity cost competitiveness, and evolving global currency dynamics in oil trade. Natural gas remains the most volatile fossil fuel when indexed historically, while solar and onshore wind present significantly lower costs for new electricity generation compared to coal and gas. Simultaneously, shifts in oil trade currencies highlight adjustments in global trade patterns, even as the US dollar retains a leading role.
